perturbation.original_text: shall not be copied, used, distributed, disclosed, disseminated or communicated in any way or form by CONSULTANT whether or not for its own benefit, to anyone outside or within its own organization, except on a "need-to-know" basis to the extent necessary for: (i) negotiations, discussions and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of NYNEX; (ii) supplying NYNEX with products or services at its order; (iii) preparing bids, estimates and proposals for submission to NYNEX; and (iv) any other purpose which NYNEX may authorize in writing
perturbation.changed_text: shall not be copied, used, distributed, disclosed, disseminated or communicated in any way or form by CONSULTANT whether or not for its own benefit, to anyone outside or within its own organization, except when reasonably believed necessary for: (i) negotiations, discussions and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of NYNEX; (ii) supplying NYNEX with products or services at its order; (iii) preparing bids, estimates and proposals for submission to NYNEX; and (iv) any other purpose which NYNEX may authorize in writing
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts the dissemination of information to a "need-to-know" basis. The changed text uses "reasonably believed necessary" which is ambiguous and subjective, allowing for broader interpretation and potential breaches of confidentiality that would not be defensible under the original clause. This creates a potential violation of data privacy regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 899-aa mandates reasonable security measures to protect private information. By allowing disclosure based on a 'reasonably believed necessary' standard, the modified text weakens the obligation to maintain strict confidentiality, potentially leading to disclosures that would be deemed unreasonable and non-compliant with the law's requirement for robust data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Business Law § 899-AA (2022) - Notification; Person Without Valid Authorization Has Acquired Private Information. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GBS - General Business Article 39-F - Notification of Unauthorized Acquisition of Private Information; Data Security Protections 899-AA - Notification; Person Without Valid Authorization Has Acquired Private Information. Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 899-AA (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, secu
perturbation.justification: The change from "need-to-know" to "reasonably believed necessary" introduces a significant ambiguity that weakens the obligation to maintain strict confidentiality. NY GBL § 899-aa, as referenced in the Law Explanation, mandates reasonable security measures to protect private information. The original contract language with "need-to-know" provides a more objective and defensible standard. The changed language introduces a subjective element, potentially allowing for disclosures that would not be considered reasonable under the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'private information' includes personal information in combination with data elements when not encrypted. The altered clause increases the risk of unauthorized access to such information, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: shall be held by CONSULTANT in the strictest confidence, and shall be treated by it with the same degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party as is used with respect to CONSULTANT'S own information of like importance, or, a minimum, shall be treated with a reasonable degree of care to avoid any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: shall be held by CONSULTANT in confidence, and shall be treated by it with a degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party, as generally practiced in the industry.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'strictest confidence' and at least 'reasonable degree of care'. The modified version weakens this by only requiring confidence and care 'as generally practiced in the industry'. This introduces ambiguity, as industry practices may vary and not meet legal requirements for data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 899-aa requires reasonable security for private information. By changing the standard of care to 'as generally practiced in the industry,' the modified text permits a lower standard of data protection than may be considered reasonable under the law, especially if industry practices are lax or outdated.

As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, secu
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates a 'strictest confidence' and at least a 'reasonable degree of care,' while the modified text lowers the standard to 'as generally practiced in the industry.' This creates a contradiction because industry standards may not meet the 'reasonable security' required by New York General Business Law § 899-aa, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippets, particularly snippet 2, reference NY Gen Bus L § 899-AA, which pertains to data security and notification requirements, suggesting that the lowered standard could potentially lead to non-compliance if industry practices are less stringent than what the law considers 'reasonable.'

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has endeavored to bind its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear obligation to ensure employees, agents, and subcontractors are bound by the NDA. The change weakens this to an 'endeavor', which doesn't guarantee compliance. This can contradict data protection regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 899-aa requires reasonable security measures. By weakening the obligation to ensure that all personnel are bound by confidentiality agreements, the modified text creates a loophole that could compromise data security and violate the law's requirements for comprehensive data protection.

As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, secu
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated that the consultant ensure its employees, agents, and subcontractors were bound by the agreement. The modified text weakens this obligation to an 'endeavor,' which does not guarantee compliance. NY GBL § 899-aa requires reasonable security measures. By weakening the obligation to ensure that all personnel are bound by confidentiality agreements, the modified text creates a loophole that could compromise data security and violate the law's requirements for comprehensive data protection. The law snippets refer to data security protections required under NY law. The change from 'warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound' to 'CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has endeavored to bind' significantly weakens the obligation, making it more difficult to enforce and potentially non-compliant with data protection regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of such request, CONSULTANT also shall erase or destroy any such Confidential Information in any computer memory or data storage apparatus.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of such request, CONSULTANT shall make commercially reasonable efforts to erase or destroy any such Confidential Information in any computer memory or data storage apparatus.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the consultant to destroy all data. The changed text replaces this with 'commercially reasonable efforts', which is a vague term that introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what level of effort is considered commercially reasonable, potentially leading to incomplete data destruction.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 899-aa mandates reasonable security measures. Introducing 'commercially reasonable efforts' allows for the possibility that not all confidential information is destroyed, which would not meet the standard of reasonable data security required under the law.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the consultant to 'erase or destroy' confidential information. The changed text introduces 'commercially reasonable efforts,' which weakens this obligation. NY GBL § 899-aa, as explained, requires reasonable security measures. 'Commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to incomplete data destruction, and failing to meet the 'reasonable security' standard required by the law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'Data Security Protections' under Article 39-F, reinforcing the legal emphasis on data security, which is undermined by the ambiguous wording in the modified text.
